coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensors for monitoring water quality: Recent advances in graphene quantum dot-based materials for the detection of toxic heavy metal ions Cd(II), Pb(II) and Hg(II) with their mechanistic aspects

coredata.dc:description: In this review, we discuss the current state of the art of electrochemical (EC) sensors based on graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based materials for the detection of heavy metal ions (HMIs), Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II). The majority of the water sources are polluted with HMIs according to WHO, and UNEP. Because of its widespread nature and high toxicity even at very low concentrations and bio accumulative properties, it is imperative to monitor and measure the concentrations of the HMIs in water sources for a safe and healthy environment. Among other HMIs, Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) bagged much attention in the research field due to its high toxicity, environmental persistence, and significant health hazards, even at trace levels and hence they are listed in the top ten highly toxic species by WHO. Thus, the EC Sensing of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) become more prevalent in water quality monitoring. Herein, we discuss the recent advances in GQD-based EC sensors for HMIs with focus on the sensors for Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II). We have further conceptualized the mechanism involved in the EC sensing. In brief, this review, we started off by emphasizing the necessity of water quality monitoring, followed by the significance of employing EC sensing in water quality assessment and the available GQD sensors in the detection of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II). The review is concluded with a detailed and critical analysis of the mechanism involved in the EC sensing process and its future perspectives.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensors for monitoring water quality: Recent advances in graphene quantum dot-based materials for the detection of toxic heavy metal ions Cd(II), Pb(II) and Hg(II) with their mechanistic aspects

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this review, we discuss the current state of the art of electrochemical (EC) sensors based on graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based materials for the detection of heavy metal ions (HMIs), Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II). The majority of the water sources are polluted with HMIs according to WHO, and UNEP. Because of its widespread nature and high toxicity even at very low concentrations and bio accumulative properties, it is imperative to monitor and measure the concentrations of the HMIs in water sources for a safe and healthy environment. Among other HMIs, Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) bagged much attention in the research field due to its high toxicity, environmental persistence, and significant health hazards, even at trace levels and hence they are listed in the top ten highly toxic species by WHO. Thus, the EC Sensing of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) become more prevalent in water quality monitoring. Herein, we discuss the recent advances in GQD-based EC sensors for HMIs with focus on the sensors for Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II). We have further conceptualized the mechanism involved in the EC sensing. In brief, this review, we started off by emphasizing the necessity of water quality monitoring, followed by the significance of employing EC sensing in water quality assessment and the available GQD sensors in the detection of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II). The review is concluded with a detailed and critical analysis of the mechanism involved in the EC sensing process and its future perspectives.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water pollution monitoring: the state of art
ce:section.ce:para: Rapid industrialization and unimpeded ejection of pollutants into water bodies have led to a severe deterioration in the accessibility of hygienic drinking water, posing a substantial threat to human and environmental health [1]. Amongst the plethora of pollutants, HMIs are among the most harmful contributors to water pollution due to their extreme toxicity, long-lasting nature, and propensity to bioaccumulate and biomagnify along the food chain [2]. Exposure to HMIs, even at trace levels, can cause severe health disorders, including neurological, renal, and cardiovascular complications [3]. Anthropogenic activities such as industrial effluents, mining, smelting, electroplating, pesticide use, and improper waste disposal contribute significantly to HMI pollution in water sources ( Fig. 1A) [4]. The toxic HMIs comprise, mercury (Hg), cadmium (Cd), lead (Pb), chromium, arsenic, and copper, have been recognized as hazardous by international regulatory bodies. These contaminants can persist in the environments for long, owing to their non-biodegradability, making their detection and amputation critical [5]. The Fig. 1B represents the permitted bounds of certain HMIs in drinking water as set by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (US-EPA).

According to UN estimates, 300–500 million tonnes of pollutants are annually entered into the world's water systems from industrial activity of which heavy metals constitute a significant share. Amongst the HMIs, Cd (II), Pb (II), and Hg (II), owing to their higher degree of toxicity, and extensive use in industries, fall in the category of priority metals of public health concern as their slower excretion from the body leads to interruption of many biological processes. Therefore, contamination by HMIs such as Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) has received increased attention. The concentrations of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) in various water sources often surpass the permissible limits set by governing bodies due to unceasing anthropogenic actions. This alarming trend, as illustrated in Fig. 2, underscores the urgent necessity for effectual and reliable monitoring techniques to detect and quantify these noxious metal ions in environmental water sources. In this scenario, to mitigate their harmful impact of HMIs on public health and the environment, electrochemical (EC) sensors have emerged as a prevailing solution with its simple, rapid, and sensitive real-time detection capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EC sensors as a promising tool for water quality analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the rising threat of the presence of HMIs in the majority of water resources, implementation of sensitive and efficient detection methods is paramount. Considering the vastness of water monitoring to achieve comprehensive coverage, the focus should be on creating cost-effective sensors that are simple to make yet reliable and rapid. The very low permissible level of HMIs calls for materials with high sensitivity, selectivity, and low limits of detection (LOD) values. EC sensing is one of the most suitable sensing platforms because of its fast response, accuracy, reliability, simplicity, user-friendliness, and cost-effectiveness. The above-mentioned characteristics led to the innovations such as glucose sensors, humidity sensors, oxygen sensors, etc., which are extremely popular and have benefitted human lives to a huge extent [6].

The current commercially available metal ion technologies, such as laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy [7], atomic absorption spectroscopy [8], X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy [9], spectrofluorimetric [10], and mass spectroscopy [11], are highly expensive, complex, non-portable, and requires multiple time-consuming steps: sample collection, pre-sampling and analysis [7–10] and expert personnel to operate the system, which make them unfit for large scale and real-time monitoring applications. In this context, EC sensing embraces notable advantages, for the onsite investigation of HMIs in the environment. The unique features of EC sensors, for instance high sensitivity, often reaching nanomolar or even femtomolar levels, making it ideal for clinical diagnostics and environmental monitoring. Most importantly, it is feasible to integrate into a miniaturized hand-held portable device [12,13], that can be extended for real-time monitoring of water. However, EC sensors hold some disadvantages as well. The main challenges include interference, electrode fouling, and matrix effects. Environmental samples often contain other metal ions and organic contaminants that can interfere with electrochemical signals, that can further reduce the accuracy. Moreover, some of these HMIs can cause electrode surface fouling, and thereby reducing sensor’s lifespan. Similarly, the real-world samples such as, industrial wastewater, biological fluids, and agricultural run-oof water, to name a few, contains potential interfering substances that can alter the variables pH and temperature. These variations can also affect the EC detection of HMIs adversely. These critical challenges have to be overcome, and that can be achieved through proper modification of the working electrode with suitable material [14]. Electrode modification materials play a crucial role in improving sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and anti-fouling properties in electrochemical sensing. The suitability of the materials falls in the criterion includes highly sensitive electrode materials that are cost-effective, easy to synthesize, reproducible for large-scale, and process to meet the requirements of sensing HMIs in water sources. The material should exhibit high electrical conductivity to facilitate efficient electron transfer between the analyte and the electrode. The modification material should provide strong binding sites for HMIs, enhancing pre-concentration and selectivity [15]. The material should resist contamination or fouling by organic molecules, biofilms, or oxide/hydroxide deposition. The material should provide a stable electrochemical response across a wide potential range, especially for simultaneous detection of multiple heavy metals.

Here comes the potentiality of carbon-based nanomaterial, especially graphene quantum dots (GQD) -modified sensors [16–18], because of their unique features, such as high surface-to-volume ratio, high electrical conductivity, and inertness. GQDs become a highly promising for heavy metal ion EC sensing due to their excellent conductivity, electron richness, high surface area, catalytic activity, and strong metal ion affinity. These unique features of GQD can satisfy almost all criterion mentioned in the above paragraph for the suitability as a modifying material for EC sensing of HMIs. Further optimization in electrode fabrication and functionalization, they offer a powerful platform for developing next-generation ultra-sensitive and portable sensors. Therefore, in this review, we focus on the EC sensors developed from GQD-based materials for the sensing of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) owing to their wide presence in water sources. EC sensors, which are based on the electrical signal derived for an analyte, are classified into various types ( Fig. 3). An EC sensor consists of two parts: a recognition layer, and a transducer: the recognition layer is formed of the material of interest used to modify the sensor. The transducer measures the current response of an analyte as a function of an applied potential. If the material interacts selectively with an analyte, then the sensor can selectively detect the analyte, and sometimes selectivity to multiple analytes/pollutants, resulting in simultaneous sensing. Electrochemical sensors have seen significant progress in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, and versatility now-a-days [19–21]. Diverse materials like carbon-based materials, GQD, MXenes, carbon nitrides, carbon nano tubes, metal oxides, etc, have been used for designing EC sensors to gain excellent sensing characteristics [22–25]. Wearable sensors are becoming increasingly important due to their ability to monitor physiological and environmental parameters [12]. Also, multiplexed sensing has become more crucial due to the practical relevance of sensors. Integrating AI and machine learning algorithms with EC sensors has made it possible to process large amounts of data, enabling reliable predictions [26–28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD as an EC sensor material
ce:section.ce:para: The emergence of nanomaterials such as advanced functional materials [25,29], inorganic quantum dots [30,31], nanospheres[32,33], metal nanoparticles [34,35]; and carbon nanomaterials, [36–39]had a crucial role in developing high-performance devices. Among them, carbon nanomaterials are one of the most explored materials, and their evolution is depicted in Fig. 4.

Though graphene was discovered in 1947 (Fig. 4), the surge of research in graphene followed after its successful isolation. Though graphene [24,25,40–46], holds many outstanding properties, it has certain limitations for many applications in semiconductor electronics, optoelectronic devices, printing electronics, photovoltaics, etc. The absence of bandgap makes it difficult to tune the semi-conductivity, while low absorptivity makes it unsuitable for optical devices. Since graphene is not soluble in many of the solvents, inks or coatings made out of it remain completely unprocessed, which led to the graphene derivatives era and the discovery of graphene quantum dots (GQD) in 2008. Since then, the number of experimental and theoretical studies of GQDs has skyrocketed [37,40–52] ( Fig. 5) owing to their size-dependent, quantum confinement and edge effects which gives rise to photoluminescence and non-zero electronic band gap [53]. The sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms and functional groups based on oxygen, which are located at the edges in GQDs, enable them to be soluble in water and other solvents, thus imparting solvent processability and providing them with sites for functionalization. GQDs act as electron donors and acceptors due to their graphene structure and edge functionalities, respectively. These properties have led to the applications of GQDs in various fields such as biosensing [54–56], bioimaging [48,57], photocatalysis [41,48], solar cells [58], energy storage and conversion [59], drug delivery [60], and nanomedicine [61] and is depicted in Fig. 6A and has created immense attention to GQD in the application-based research areas in this short span of time.

In addition to its unique characteristic properties, compared to other carbon nanomaterials, further enhancement or tailoring in the characteristics properties of GQDs can be achieved through surface functionalization or doping with heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N) [36,62–67], sulfur (S) [68–72], boron (B) [19,50,73–76], and phosphorous (P)[71,77,78] to suit for various applications. Surface functionalization and heteroatom doping can change various properties of GQDs, such as their electronic, conductive, catalytic, and photoluminescence characteristics. The possible or reported doping patterns of GQDs are represented in Fig. 6B, and their characteristic features are discussed in the following sections.

The similarity in the atomic radius of N-atom (0.75 Aº) with C-atom (0.77 Aº) and the greater electronegativity (χ N = 3.04) of N than C (χc = 2.55), making it easier to incorporate into the carbon network69, has resulted in N- doped GQD being one of the widely studied GQD, a general structural representation of N-GQD is given in Fig. 7A. The possible doping sites of N in GQDs are (i) graphitic N, where N-atoms are substituted in the hexagonal ring of graphene, (ii) pyrrolic, and (iii) pyridinic [64], where N is found at the edge defects. However, the hybridization of pyridinic N is sp2 whereas pyrrolic N is sp3 hybridized. The difference in the electronegativity imparted due to the doping of N has led to an enhancement in electronic and conductivity properties that, in turn, led to enhanced sensing, energy storage, and catalysis properties.

The S atom (1.05 Aº) being much larger than the C atom and the two atoms having different outermost orbitals, the doping of S in GQDs results in an uneven spin density distribution. The electronegativity values of S (2.58) and C (2.55) are similar, suggesting that the driving force for electron transfer between C and S is comparatively low [79]. These characteristics make S doping into a GQD framework difficult, whereas the doping can lead to enhanced electronic, catalytic, and emissive properties due to the extension of conjugation and the insertion of non-bonded electrons into the system, a representative structure of S-GQD is given in Fig. 7B. The S doping happens in the aromatic basal planes as thiophene or rather added as surface functionalities as oxides of S [69,80].

The B (0.84 Aº) has a similar atomic size as that of C (0.77 Aº) and the electronegativity values of C and B are 2.55 and 2.04, respectively. The difference in the electronegativities of C and B can result in charge transfer among these and thereby enhancing its conductivity properties [79]. The B-doping is also termed hole doping, in response to the development of holes or defects on GQD due to B-doping [73]. The reports of B-GQDs are very few compared to the N-GQD and S-GQD reports because of the difficulty in the synthesis of B-GQD due to the large activation energy barrier (1.3 eV) for B-doping, a Fig. 7C shows the representative structure of B-GQD.

The synthesis of P-GQDs is energetically more favorable than N-doping because of the lower C-P formation energy (7.1 eV/atom) to that of the (8.0 eV/atom) for C-N. However, the larger size of the P-atom (98 pm) compared to that of C(70 pm) makes the doping difficult. The bandgap of P-doped one (0.53 eV) is higher than that of the N-doped (0.14 eV) for the same doping concentration. The P-doped GQDs can also show the characteristics of p-type semiconductors [79], a representative structure of P-GQD is given in Fig. 7D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs with more than one heteroatom doping
ce:section.ce:para: Further, it was observed that co-doping of GQD [71,72,77,80–82] can improve or impart properties by generating additional coordination sites and surface functionalities and providing more structural defects. The reported co-doping of GQDs includes the various combinations of B, N, P and S. Due to the similar atomic size, co-doping of GQD with N and B is easier, where N provides n-type (electron) doping, and B provides p-type (hole) doping. The electro-negative values of C (2.55), N (3.04), and B (2.04) have sufficient differences that can provide charge transfer between the C–N/C–B bond. In the case of P and S co-doped GQDs, the synthesis and formation are complex since P and S are larger atoms compared to C. Also, the electro-negative values of P (2.19), S (2.58), and C (2.55) are similar, which leads to an insignificant charge transfer between C-P and C-S bonds. A representative structure of co-doped GQDs is given in Fig. 7E-H.

The integration of heteroatoms (such as N, S, B, and P) into GQDs has emerged as an incredible approach for enhancing the EC sensing performance of HMI sensors. This is because, the electronic structure, surface chemistry and functionalities, and also the conductivity properties of GQDs can be altered significantly through heteroatom doping, thereby leading to improved sensitivity, selectivity, and stability in HMI detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Significance of EC sensing of toxic metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Industrial and other activities have made the presence of toxic metal ions of cadmium (Cd), lead (Pb), mercury (Hg), and arsenic prevalent in groundwater and open water sources like lakes, rivers, streams, and wells ( Fig. 8). Studies and statistics point to Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) as major contributors (∼37 %) towards global environmental pollution [85]. The lethal characteristics, such as inimical toxicity, widespread use, low degradability, and high bio-accumulative property, made their constant monitoring of water sources imminent. Also, the HMIs Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II), has made to the list of top 10 chemicals of primary public health concern by the World Health Organization [2], along with arsenic, asbestos, benzene, dioxins, fluoride, highly hazardous pesticides and air pollutants.

Hg(II) is considered one of the most neurotoxic and hazardous environmental pollutants due to its persistence in the environment owing to its reactive nature, and it can bind to various chemical species in the environment and form methyl Hg (CH3Hg+) and become soluble in water. In the water bodies Hg(II) accumulates through the food chain, thus posing a serious threat to humans. Hg(II) can severely affect almost all major human body organs and can cause serious damage to the heart, kidneys, lungs, brain development of the foetus, and the immune system. The diseases such as pink disease and Minamata disease are also caused by severe Hg poisoning. The toxicity of Hg (II) is evident from its safe limit of ≤ 2 ppb in drinking water, prescribed by the USEPA [1].

The wide use of Cd in industrial applications has led to the widespread presence of Cd(II) in the environment. The maximum permissible Cd(II) limit in drinking water is ≤ 5 ppb as per the USEPA [83], and intakes higher than this can result in heart diseases, emphysema, hypertension, reproductive failure, cancer, and Itai-itai disease and damage to the central nervous system, renal system and DNA.

In the case of Pb(II), the maximum acceptable concentration of Pb(II) is ≤ 15 ppb in drinking water. Pb(II) being so widely used in rechargeable batteries, electroplating, bullets, soldering, protective shielding for nuclear reactors, particle accelerators, X-ray equipment, containers used for transporting and storing radioactive materials and their non-proper disposal has led to the contamination of land and majority of water sources around the globe with Pb(II). Being non-biodegradable makes the possibility of its presence higher in water sources. It causes greater risks to human health [86] including damage to the liver, kidney, skin, skeletal system, nervous and haematological systems and can reside up to ≤ 12 years in the human body due to bio-accumulation ( Fig. 9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Why EC sensing of toxic metal ions?
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the adverse effects, proper detection strategies and continuous monitoring of heavy metals in environmental bodies are crucial to protect the health of the environment and human beings. Present determination of the HMIs in the ecological samples is achieved through techniques that demand bulkier and expensive instrumentation, frequent sample collection, pre-sampling, and expert personnel for their operation and time-consuming analysis and, hence, are not suitable for on-field analysis or continuous monitoring. A comparison of the reports of the techniques for the detection of Cd(II), Pb(II) and Hg(II) is given in Table 1. From the data (Table 1), it can be noted that of the possible portable methods, such as EC, fluorescence, SERS, and field effect transistors, EC sensing has a fast response time (the time of analysis being ≤10 s), combined with its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, user-friendliness, portability with no or minimal pre-sampling. Thus, EC sensing is an appealing method for continuous monitoring and on-site detection of trace HMIs in environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD-based EC sensors for Hg(II), Pb(II) and Cd(II) detection
ce:section.ce:para: This review focuses on the GQD-based EC sensors due to the remarkable properties of GQD, as discussed in Section 1. Due to quantum confinement and edge effect in GQD, they possess high-speed electron transport and excellent conductivity, [105] making the GQDs an exceptional platform for high-speed electron transfer reactions. In addition, the higher number of edges and defects can facilitate electro-catalysing, which further helps increase the signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio in sensors and biosensors, and enhance the sensing performance in GQD-based sensors.

Among the reports of GQDs-based EC sensors for the detection of Hg(II), an amino-functionalised GQD and poly(thionine) nanocomposite was utilized, which achieved a LOD of 0.6 pM and a linear range of 1 pM to 1 µM [106]. The sensor demonstrated analysis in real water samples successfully, even without pre-concentration. In another work, Kala et al. developed a molecular imprinted GQD, which is functionalised using thiourea derivative for the selective sensing of Hg(II) ions using both CV and DPV analyses. In the case of CV studies, the sensor showed a linear response in the range of 6 × 10–8 to 8.5 × 10–7 M and 1.4 × 10–6 to 8 × 10–6 M with a LOD of 30.2 nM, and in DPV, the linear range was from 5 × 10–8 M to 2.3 × 10–5 M with a LOD of 23.5 nM, whereas the non-functionalised did not show the sensing of the Hg(II) [107]. The reports show that modification of GQDs with suitable functionalities can improve the sensing properties of the sensor in terms of sensitivity and LOD. For instance, Chen et al., synthesised an AuNP-modified GQD electrode for the EC detection of Hg(II) ions via anodic stripping procedure [108], which achieved a LOD of 0.02 nM with a sensitivity of 2.47 µA/nM. Thus, incorporating AuNP could improve the LOD to 1000 times lower than the amino and thiourea functionalized GQD. This is due to the synergistic effect of both the affinity between Au-Hg and the electrochemistry of the AuNP-modified GQD system with Hg(II). Similarly, the effective N-doping through in situ N present in PANI for the synthesis of N-GQD [83] was demonstrated by Sandhya et al. that can selectively detect Cd(II) with a sensitivity of 3.570 and 0.074 μAμM−1 cm−2 whereas the undoped (GQD) could not. The N-GQD on co-doping with S, which was referred to as S,N-GQD [69] showed simultaneous sensing of Hg(II), Pb(II) and Cd(II), with enhanced current response for Cd(II). The S co-doping of N-GQD, resulted in exhibiting the LOD of 1 pM for Hg(II) with a linear dynamic range of 10−10 to 10−4 M and displayed high sensitivity values of 12, 13, and 5 μAμM−1 cm−2, whereas N-GQD was not able to sense Hg(II) and Pb(II). Kenneth D et al., prepared a N-GQD [18] with high amidation via IR-assisted induction heating method of citric acid and urea. The sensor had a linear range of 0.05–0.25 μM with a LOD of 0.05 µM. Here it can be observed that the in situ doped N-GQD showed pM level detection, whereas N-GQD synthesised with an external doping agent exhibited LOD in the µM range. This is assigned to the homogeneous effective doping in the aromatic plane in situ synthesis. A comparison among the EC sensing capability of GQD over other materials for the sensing of Hg(II) is given in Table 2. From the table, it is well clear that GQD/GQD-based materials exhibited improved sensing in terms of sensitivity, and LOD. This is because of the presence of electron-rich aromatic basal planes in GQD along with the non-bonded electrons, which provide a better conductive platform, and the functional groups present at the periphery of GQDs, which create affinity points that can interact with or adsorb the analytes for the modified sensors. The Fig. 10 shows the reported DPV responses of certain modified GCE towards Hg(II), Cd(II) and Pb(II) [22,109].

In the case of Cd(II), the GQD-based sensors by Sandhya et al. [83] showed two linear ranges: from 10−11 to 10−4 M and 10−4 to 10−3 M with an experimental LOD of 10−13 M, whereas the group's S, N-GQD [69] exhibited a linear detection range of 10−10 to 10−4 M with LOD of 10−12 M. Chen et al. reported [109] a silica nanochannel grafted GQDs where vertically-ordered mesoporous silica-nanochannel film was grown on ITO electrode using Stöber solution-growth approach, and could be used for the determination of Cd(II). The sensor had anti-fouling and anti-interference properties through steric exclusion and electrostatic repulsion by the aromatic graphitic planes of GQD. The sensor exhibited an LOD of 4.3 nM and a linear range of 1–20 µM.

Kong et al. [110] synthesised a nanocomposite EC sensing platform consisting of chitosan and GQD, which was modified using in-situ plated Bi film to determine Pb(II). The sensor showed a linear response in the range of 50–450 ppb with a LOD 3.1 ppb. Zhou et al., [17] demonstrated a greener approach in synthesising GQDs using graphene oxide, polystyrene sulfonate and L-ascorbic acid (L-AA) for selective sensing of Pb(II). The response had a linear range of 8 × 10−7 to1 × 10−5 M with an LOD of 7 pM. An EC sensor for Pb(II) [111] detection was synthesized from d-penicillamine functionalised GQDs by Ahour et al. The LOD was found to be 0.01 nM with a linear range of 0.03–2.8 nM. In another work by Sandhya et al. a S, N-GQD [69] which detects Pb(II) ions with a linear range of 10−10 to 10−4 M and a LOD of 10−11 M was reported.

From Table 2 and the reports, it can be observed in general that there is an enhanced sensing ability in the GQD-based EC sensors [44] with excellent LOD and LDR ranges compared to other materials such as inorganic quantum dots [78], MOF [22], inorganic-organic composites [112]. Another notable feature of GQD-based sensors is that they do not require anodic stripping due to their electron-rich graphene structure. In contrast, other EC sensor materials had to employ [113,114] the stripping technique which comprises a pre-reduction step [115] for a certain optimized time to facilitate the sensing, as no spontaneous reduction was observed or in an attempt to enhance the sensitivity and the LOD values. The stripping technique is a time-consuming process and hence is a drawback for the real-time applicability of the sensor, as this may require trial and error steps to determine the time required for different samples, and makes the sensing studies less simple[2]. Further, the hetero atom doping was able to enhance the sensing ability, which was assigned to the incorporation of non-bonded electrons. The enhanced catalytic activities due to the hetero atom functionalities and the introduction of sites of different electronegativities enable improved interactions with either electropositive or electronegative atoms in the analytes [83,66,116]. For instance, N-GQD was found to be better than GQD for the EC sensing of many of the toxic metal ions [83] because of the insertion of N functionalities, such as pyridinic, pyrrolic, and oxidised pyridinic [64], which in turn provides sites for interaction with its non-bonded electrons or creates affinity sites which can interact via soft-soft or hard-hard acid-base interactions or other means of ionic interactions for the analytes. Further, such dopings (S or N) increased the electronic and conductivity properties and improved catalytic properties in N-GQD [117], S,N-GQD [69], B,P-GQD and N,B-GQD [78]. This can further facilitate simultaneous sensing, excellent sensitivity and lower LOD [69,83]. The DPV responses of certain modified GCE towards simultaneous sensing of Hg(II), Cd(II) and Pb(II) is shown in Fig. 11.

A comparison of the EC sensing capability of different GQD-based sensors for selective and simultaneous detection of Cd(II), Pb(II) and Hg(II) are given in Tables 2 and 3, respectively. The Tables 2 and 3 shows that the GQD-based materials have remarkable sensing capability towards Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) compared to other materials, such as multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWCNT), metal-organic frameworks (MOF), inorganic QDs and other organic-inorganic composite materials. Different techniques, such as square-wave anodic stripping voltammetry (SWASV), differential pulse stripping voltammetry (DPSV), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), have been utilized for investigating the sensing properties. In SV techniques, the pre-reduction step is used for the reduction of metal ions. This is due to the oxidized state of the metal ions in the electrolyte solution, and the spontaneous reduction on the electrode material fails, or to improve the sensitivity and LOD values of the sensor. The pre-reduction step can increase the total time of analysis since this process requires a certain period of time (≥ 30 minutes) of applying voltage before conducting the sensing experiment compared to the normal DPV. However, in the case of GQD-based sensors, the pre-reduction can be avoided because of its ability to reduce the metal ions due to its electron-rich nature and hence the analysis time can be very fast (in seconds). Further, the capability of reducing the metal ions spontaneously on the surface of GQDs leads to excellent sensitivity and low LOD values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the EC sensing of HMIs by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: It can be observed that the sensing of toxic HMIs, either selectively or simultaneously can be tuned by doping of GQD by different atoms. GQDs can sense toxic metal ions such as Hg (II), Pb(II) and Cd(II) by interacting with the respective ions via surface functional groups [69]. As per the existing reports, the charge transfer, the binding energy, and the equilibrium distances between the GQD and the analyte metal ions are found to be strongly dependent on the surface functionalities, the charge state of the metal ions, and the size/edge geometry of the GQDs. In GQD, the interaction is mainly due to the surface phenolic/ hydroxyl group [147]. Surface functionalization and modifications are done to enhance the sensing activity by doping with the desired species. The characteristics of the dopant atom depend on the nature of the analyte. Other forms of functionalisation include forming composites with inorganic materials or polymers, and controlling of size and shape of GQDs [148,149].

Metal ion sensing without the pre-concentration step requires both the adsorption of metal ions to the surface and an electron-rich surface, which can spontaneously reduce the Mn+ to M0 state, as observed [69]. Heteroatom doping is an effective method for enhancing the interaction between GQD and the analytes [83]. The interactions that cause sensing are mainly due to the attractive forces between the sensor and the analyte. The mechanism by which they interact is reported to follow various pathways. Fu et al. suggest a two-step mechanism for detecting Hg(II) by GQD hybrid electrodes, including ionic adsorption followed by an electrochemical redox reaction [18]. The stepwise redox reaction is expressed in Fig. 12.

The reactions that enable the sensing action thus can follow multiple pathways depending on the surface properties ( Fig. 13A). C. Zhou et al. synthesized GQD with sodium polystyrene sulfonate as a dispersant, making the surface more negatively charged, and the existence of charge is beneficial for the adsorption of metal ions [17] (Fig. 13B). Sithara et al. synthesized a covalently modified GQD using a thiourea-based imprinted polymer for the selective EC sensing of Hg(II) ions. N and S functionalization using thiourea precursor is expected to make the surface electron-rich, forming specific sites for the adsorption and coordination of Hg(II). This study also supports the mechanism of adsorption followed by reduction [107]. Liang Tan et al. modified the GCE with nanocomposites consisting of poly(thionine) and amino-functionalized GQDs, which were effectively oxidized by hydroxyl radical generated by copper-catalyzed Fenton-like reaction leading to a displacement reaction involving Hg(II) that mediates the detection of the Hg(II) in the water sample without the pre-concentration procedure. Similarly, a N-GQD synthesized from polyaniline exhibited specific Cd(II) detection without pre-reduction [83] due to the soft acid - soft base interaction between the pyridinic and pyrrolic N and Cd(II) (Fig. 13)

It was evident that the addition of heteroatom caused remarkable changes in the EC sensing properties. For instance, while N-GQDPANI [83] detected Cd(II) alone, when co-doped with S; N, S-GQDPANI [69] detected Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) The sensing of Pb(II) and Hg(II) happened because of their adsorption /affinity to S-atoms, which brought them to the surface of GQDs and caused their spontaneous reduction (Fig. 13D). The positive charges created by donating electrons are stabilized by the electron-releasing groups in the aromatic skeleton of the S,N-GQDPANI. F. Ahour et al. modified GQD with D-Penicillamine, which imparted O, S, and N functionality along with enhanced surface area, resulting in the effective sensing of Pb(II)196. The presence of thiol, amine, and carboxylic acid chelating groups on the DPA structure resulted in the adsorption of more Pb(II) ions at the surface. Functionalized GQDs confined to the vertically-ordered mesoporous silica-nanochannel film on an ITO electrode were used to detect HMIs from complex samples [150]. Here, the functionalized GQD serves as the recognition and signal amplifier through interaction with analytes to attain enrichment and facilitate charge transfer. Hwang et al. prepared chitosan-GQD (CS-GQD) combined with in-situ Bi(III) film for sensing of (Pb(II), Cd(II) & Zn(II)) where GQDs were well dispersed in chitosan matrix due to electrostatic forces between positively charged CS and negatively charged GQD [151]. The water insolubility contributed to the stability of the composite, which further enhanced its stability. Another factor is the easy adsorption of metal ions onto highly porous CS-GQD. GQDs decorated with nanoparticles or nanoclusters are also used in the EC sensing of HMIs. Siong et al. used GQDs functionalized onto cysteamine-capped AuNPs for the EC detection of Hg(II) and Cu(II)192 (Fig. 13E), exploiting the affinity of Au towards Hg(II) and the interaction of the surface carboxyl group present in the GQD.

Thus, from the observations, it can be seen that the sensing mechanism is based on the strength and the modes of interaction between the GQD and the analyte. In some cases, if it is the electron affinity, in others, it is the soft acid-soft base interaction or electrostatic or affinity to certain heteroatoms or other possible interactions leading to the adsorption of metal ions followed by a spontaneous reduction reaction in some cases due to the electron rich graphene which then will appear as an oxidation peak during the oxidation potential sweep, thus ensuring a change in the current, resulting in the sensing. In cases of no spontaneous reduction, pre-reduction step become essential. While Hg(II) and Cd(II) are Lewis soft acids, Pb(II) falls under the intermediate category. Though the GQD is electron-rich, N-doping enhances its electron availability and gives its interacting/functional groups a soft base character. Further, the soft acid-soft base interaction leads to the sensing of Hg(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II), as evident in the discussion.

Researchers often prefer nanomaterials with a large specific surface area [20,152–154] for designing sensing interfaces, as effective metal ion adsorption is a crucial prerequisite for initiating succeeding electrochemical reactions. The high surface area enhances the interaction between the analyte and the sensor, in that way improving sensitivity and detection efficiency. In EC sensing, the process of redox reactions is also a catalytic reaction where electrode modification materials were act as catalysts [155]. Thus, the detection of HMIs is getting boosted by modification with suitable material, indicating improved rates of conversion and the redox reaction. This is because nanomaterials are abundant in catalytic active sites, that serve as redox mediators and the electrons from nanomaterials can facilitate HMIs for spontaneous reduction on its surface. Consequently, the description of catalysis in the electroanalysis of HMIs is the process of enlightening conversion rates and reaction rates, lessening the energy barriers, and thereby varying the rate-determining steps in the redox reactions under the effect of modified electrode nanomaterials [155]. Fig. 14 demonstrates the critical steps related to catalysis of EC sensing of Cd(II), Pb(II) and Hg(II) on GQD-based sensors. The main steps involved in the EC sensing of Cd(II), Pb(II) and Hg(II) on GQD involve: the specific interactions arised through hetero atom doping enabled the diffusion and the further selective adsorption of Cd(II), Pb(II) and Hg(II) by GQD modified sensors. Further, electron enriched bed of GQD can facilitate the spontaneous reduction of these HMI on its surface. That then resulted in the oxidation of Cd(II), Pb(II) and Hg(II) during their oxidation voltage sweep.

The incorporation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) with GQD-based EC sensors can pave advancement in catalytic processes and thus enhanced the detection of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II). AI-driven methods can significantly improve the sensitivity, selectivity, and data interpretation, and by this means enabling rapid and accurate detection [156]. Lack of publications that exactly addressing the integration of AI or ML with GQD-based EC sensors for detection of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II), opens an extensive study in these area. ML models can optimize the surface functionalization of GQD to improve the adsorption and electron transfer kinetics of HMIs [157]. Also, it can analyse even delicate variations in EC response, and thereby reducing false positives and false negatives. Later, the AI-assisted feature facilitates the distinction between Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) signals through background noise reduction, thereby the accuracy of data can be improved. AI models, such as principal component analysis and support vector machines, and also the deep learning techniques, such as convolutional neural networks can be utilised for the enhanced detection of multiple HMIs through pattern prediction, detection of very low concentrations through the concentration predictions and, specific or simultaneous sensing by predicting ideal surface functionalisation to capitalize on adsorption sites.

In nutshell, the detection of Hg(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II) on GQD modified electrodes are enabled through adsorption, electrochemical deposition through its reducing capability, catalytic enhancement though hetero atom functionalities, and specificity achieved through functionalization. These being the main mechanistic aspect behind the HMI detection through GDQ-based sensors. Thus, it is evident that hetero-atom doping creates various exposed interaction sites on GQD and there exist a competition for the adsorption sites on GQD. The favorable interaction dominates, causing the sensing of different analytes either selectively or simultaneously. For instance, Pb(II) and Hg(II) have an exceptional affinity towards S; thus, effective S doping leads to the detection of Pb(II) and Hg(II) over other metal ions. Similarly, an effective N-doping created soft-base functionalities such as pyridinic and pyrrolic in the aromatic skeleton of GQD, which put forward an efficient soft base-soft acid interaction towards Cd(II), leading to its detection. The electron affinity between the metal ions and the heteroatoms paved another route for its detection and sometimes can impart selective sensing to the particular metal ion. The interactions mentioned above, along with the improved electron transfer abilities of doped GQD through the electron-rich aromatic planes (π and non-boded electrons) and conductivity of the aromatic plane, play a crucial role in the excellent EC sensing properties of heteroatom doped GQDs. Thus, the selectivity of GQDs for different metal ions can be tuned by introducing specific functional groups.

Exploring hybrid nanostructures, functionalization of GQDs with specific ligands (e.g., thiol, amine, or crown ethers), integration of nanocomposites and molecularly imprinted polymers can enhance electrode stability, enable selective binding, and tune adsorption affinity towards HMIs. The current sensing technologies are focusing on single-electrode detection. Integration with screen-printed electrodes for on-site monitoring with multi-electrode arrays enabling simultaneous detection of HMIs paving towards the successful utilisation of the sensor for commercial applicability. Further, incorporating machine learning algorithms for pattern recognition, smartphone-based electrochemical detection and the utilisation of smart technologies such as AI and IoT in sensing can upgrade these sensors to the next advanced level of real-time detection. Dual mode sensing strategies can also be explored with the integration of EC sensing with other sophisticated technologies for instance, Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy for more enhanced and accurate detection for exploring the versatility.

This review briefly discusses the EC sensing capabilities of GQD-based sensor materials as a promising vector for real-time water quality monitoring. The superior EC sensing performances of GQD-based electrodes towards the detection of Hg(II), Cd(II) and Pb(II) have been demonstrated, especially the recent progress in the heteroatom-doped GQDs. The mechanisms involved in the sensing properties of various GQDs were unravelled in detail. GQDs have emerged as promising electrode modifiers for the EC sensing of HMIs: Hg(II), Cd(II) and Pb(II) due to their excellent conductivity, high surface area, and rich surface functional groups. The detection mechanisms involve a combination of adsorption, electrochemical deposition by its electron rich nature, catalytic enhancement through hetero atom functionalities, functionalized selectivity and specific interactions such as soft acid-soft base, electrostatic interactions to name a few. Nonetheless, there is still much room for exploring GQD-based electrodes in EC sensors for HMIs and the challenges remain in understanding the exact mechanism behind the sensing. The hetero atom doping and the tuning of surface functionalities of GQD are crucial in determining the EC sensing characteristics of GQD towards HMIs since the specific molecular interactions play a vital role in selective/simultaneous detection. Also, by logically designing GQDs with hetero atoms, the sensing capabilities of the material can be tuned to achieve enhanced sensing properties. Further, even though GQD-based electrochemical sensors offer a highly sensitive, cost-effective, and scalable approach for heavy metal detection, the future research should focus on enhancing selectivity, improving stability through combinations with other suitable nanomaterials. In addition to hetero atom doped GQDs, hybrid nanostructures, including metal nanoclusters, 2D nanomaterials, molecularly imprinted polymers, to name a few, can also applied for EC sensing applications that are not explored in the area of HMIs hitherto. Miniaturization and integration with AI-based smart technologies has to be explored further to facilitate practical and commercial applications in this area.

S. Saisree: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Dais Elsa: Writing – original draft, Resources, Data curation.
